findings.specific_passage: Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR. ... If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot. If all three pass when money is actually involved, you have a feature idea, not a product.
findings.challenge_statement: What evidence here is actually representative, and what is merely vivid, recent, or easy to recall?
findings.next_move: Write the disconfirming evidence that would outweigh the salient anecdote before moving forward.
major_tensions: Salience correction is fake if the team mentions a denominator but still lets the memorable anecdote decide the case.
findings.intervention_hint: Write the disconfirming evidence that would outweigh the salient anecdote before moving forward.

chunks.text: Analysis paralysis can keep the team waiting for perfect information instead of learning through reversible movement.
Have we generated enough options before narrowing the choice set?
Commitment Bias: Commitment-and-consistency-bias makes optionality psychologically expensive because prior choices become harder to revisit even when better paths appear.
chunks.affinity_rationale: Commitment makes it 'extremely difficult to pivot or consider new, better options' — psychological pressure from prior choices directly destroys the flexibility optionality requires.
chunks.activation_condition: When prior investment or public commitment creates escalation pressure that forecloses objectively superior alternatives because pivoting feels like failure

chunks.text: Invent first and decide later so evaluation does not choke off option generation too early.

Select when: Uncertainty is high, early commitment is expensive, and cheap experiments can buy learning before an irreversible call. | Danger when: Optionality has become a permanent excuse not to commit even after evidence has clarified which path has the best economics or strategic fit. | Reasoning: counterfactual, probabilistic

anchors.evidence_quote: Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?" Pick a price. See what they say. That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer proposes a cheap, targeted experiment (pre-buy test) under high uncertainty to buy learning before the irreversible pivot commitment, directly executing the optionality mechanism.

chunks.text: The model breaks when teams keep fixing the old bottleneck after the constraint has already shifted somewhere else.
Constraint language becomes theater when no current cap, governing metric, or movement evidence is named.
Before committing: what is the single measured limit that would still cap throughput if every local improvement in this plan succeeded next week?
If this first bottleneck actually moves, what step becomes the next cap within two weeks?
Define constraints rigorously up front so the problem scope and the bottleneck candidate are explicit before solutioning.

anchors.evidence_quote: The thing that ate the month wasn't indecision about the pivot — it was avoidance of the hard conversations: with the customers who might say no, with the employee who might be laid off, with the friend who might feel excluded, with yourself about a product that isn't working. Those conversations are the bottleneck, not the strategic question.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer identifies avoidance of hard conversations as the binding constraint blocking throughput on the pivot, applying theory-of-constraints by focusing improvement there.

chunks.text: Simplification can fail by collapsing into vague slogans that no longer guide prediction, action, or alignment.
Gather only enough facts to support or falsify the decision rather than boiling the ocean for decorative certainty.
Filter explanations by purpose so only detail that improves prediction, choice, or coordination survives.
Anchoring: Anchoring makes false-precision-avoidance necessary because one precise number can dominate judgment long after it stops being the right guide.
chunks.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes how 'people focus too much on a single, specific piece of information (the anchor), often a precise number, to misleadingly influence overall judgment' — directly opposes FPA's mission.
chunks.activation_condition: When a precise number anchors judgment and misleadingly influences the overall assessment, even though the number's precision exceeds the reliability of the underlying data

chunks.text: Base Rates: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with base-rates when rounding away precision discards meaningful base-rate variation that would change the decision.

anchors.evidence_quote: before you run the pre-buy test, write down what "pass" looks like. Two of three commit? All three? What dollar amount? If you decide the bar after seeing the results, you'll negotiate with yourself into whatever answer you want. Bar first, data second.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer mandates pre-defining directional thresholds (pass bar) before data to avoid post-hoc rationalization and false precision, executing the model's mechanism.

chunks.text: A tree can look complete while still missing the threshold, dependency break, or second-order consequence that kills the favored branch.

Which branch in this tree looks viable only because we have not named the threshold that kills it, the dependency that can fail first, or the second-order consequence that makes an early win a later loss?
Which threshold assumption would reverse which branch is correct, and what evidence would tell us we have already crossed it before sunk effort locks us into the wrong path?
Define branch-kill thresholds in advance so the team knows exactly which observed condition ends the favored path instead of improvising under pressure.
Chaos Theory: Decision-trees conflicts with chaos-theory when structured branch logic meets domains dominated by non-linearity and unpredictability.

anchors.evidence_quote: If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot. If all three pass when money is actually involved, you have a feature idea, not a product.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer defines explicit decision branches with trigger conditions (2/3 pre-buy commitment) before resource commitment, instantiating decision-trees.

That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Commitment-and-consistency-bias makes optionality psychologically expensive because prior choices become harder to revisit even when better paths appear.
expansions.why_relevant: Commitment-and-consistency-bias makes optionality psychologically expensive because prior choices become harder to revisit even when better paths appear.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Commitment makes it 'extremely difficult to pivot or consider new, better options' — psychological pressure from prior choices directly destroys the flexibility optionality requires.
expansions.activation_condition: When prior investment or public commitment creates escalation pressure that forecloses objectively superior alternatives because pivoting feels like failure

expansions.substrate_chunk: Optionality conflicts with commitment-bias when preserving options delays the commitment needed to capture time-sensitive value.
expansions.why_relevant: Optionality conflicts with commitment-bias when preserving options delays the commitment needed to capture time-sensitive value.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias shrinks optionality by locking attention onto the preferred path and filtering out superior alternatives.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias shrinks optionality by locking attention onto the preferred path and filtering out superior alternatives.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Described as 'severely limiting' optionality — confirmation bias causes inability to see superior alternatives by seeking information that confirms the preferred option.
expansions.activation_condition: When attachment to a preferred option causes selective evidence gathering that makes alternatives invisible despite their objective superiority

expansions.substrate_chunk: Decision-trees conflicts with chaos-theory when structured branch logic meets domains dominated by non-linearity and unpredictability.
expansions.why_relevant: Decision-trees conflicts with chaos-theory when structured branch logic meets domains dominated by non-linearity and unpredictability.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Decision-trees conflicts with cognitive-biases when a rational branch structure is distorted by biased probability and outcome assignment.
expansions.why_relevant: Decision-trees conflicts with cognitive-biases when a rational branch structure is distorted by biased probability and outcome assignment.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Decision-trees conflicts with confirmation-bias when branch probabilities are tuned to validate the analyst's preferred path.
expansions.why_relevant: Decision-trees conflicts with confirmation-bias when branch probabilities are tuned to validate the analyst's preferred path.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Theory-of-constraints conflicts with commitment-bias when the team keeps investing in the first bottleneck story instead of reassessing whether it is still binding.
expansions.why_relevant: Theory-of-constraints conflicts with commitment-bias when the team keeps investing in the first bottleneck story instead of reassessing whether it is still binding.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias corrupts theory-of-constraints by making the team protect its favorite bottleneck story instead of measuring the real cap.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias corrupts theory-of-constraints by making the team protect its favorite bottleneck story instead of measuring the real cap.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes confirmation bias as encouraging substitution of familiar frameworks for objective constraint identification
expansions.activation_condition: When teams substitute a familiar framework for objective identification of the actual binding constraint

expansions.substrate_chunk: Theory-of-constraints conflicts with optimization-theory when local utilization improvements in non-constrained steps create the illusion of progress.
expansions.why_relevant: Theory-of-constraints conflicts with optimization-theory when local utilization improvements in non-constrained steps create the illusion of progress.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Anchoring makes false-precision-avoidance necessary because one precise number can dominate judgment long after it stops being the right guide.
expansions.why_relevant: Anchoring makes false-precision-avoidance necessary because one precise number can dominate judgment long after it stops being the right guide.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes how 'people focus too much on a single, specific piece of information (the anchor), often a precise number, to misleadingly influence overall judgment' — directly opposes FPA's mission.
expansions.activation_condition: When a precise number anchors judgment and misleadingly influences the overall assessment, even though the number's precision exceeds the reliability of the underlying data

expansions.substrate_chunk: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with base-rates when rounding away precision discards meaningful base-rate variation that would change the decision.
expansions.why_relevant: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with base-rates when rounding away precision discards meaningful base-rate variation that would change the decision.

expansions.substrate_chunk: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with bayesian when avoidance of precise probability estimates prevents the quantitative prior-updating that Bayesian reasoning requires.
expansions.why_relevant: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with bayesian when avoidance of precise probability estimates prevents the quantitative prior-updating that Bayesian reasoning requires.

failure_hints.text: Analysis paralysis can keep the team waiting for perfect information instead of learning through reversible movement.
Anchoring on the first option presented can quietly shrink the perceived option set and bias later evaluation.
A tree can look complete while still missing the threshold, dependency break, or second-order consequence that kills the favored branch.
Confirmation bias or Maslow's hammer can shape the tree around the analyst's preferred hypothesis instead of the real structure.
Constraint language becomes theater when no current cap, governing metric, or movement evidence is named.
The model breaks when teams keep fixing the old bottleneck after the constraint has already shifted somewhere else.
Teams can use simplicity to hide uncertainty and present a rounded answer as if the underlying variance no longer matters.
Simplification can fail by collapsing into vague slogans that no longer guide prediction, action, or alignment.

heuristic_hints.text: Bias toward action on reversible decisions because they buy information at low downside cost.
Invent first and decide later so evaluation does not choke off option generation too early.
Define branch-kill thresholds in advance so the team knows exactly which observed condition ends the favored path instead of improvising under pressure.
Embed explicit branch logic so the system can name when action A should beat action B.
Define constraints rigorously up front so the problem scope and the bottleneck candidate are explicit before solutioning.
Disaggregate and prioritize until only the branches affecting the binding constraint stay in focus.
Filter explanations by purpose so only detail that improves prediction, choice, or coordination survives.
Gather only enough facts to support or falsify the decision rather than boiling the ocean for decorative certainty.

premortem_hints.text: Is the worst case bad enough?
Have we generated enough options before narrowing the choice set?

Which branch in this tree looks viable only because we have not named the threshold that kills it, the dependency that can fail first, or the second-order consequence that makes an early win a later loss?
Which threshold assumption would reverse which branch is correct, and what evidence would tell us we have already crossed it before sunk effort locks us into the wrong path?
Before committing: what is the single measured limit that would still cap throughput if every local improvement in this plan succeeded next week?
If this first bottleneck actually moves, what step becomes the next cap within two weeks?
If this was cut, would anything be lost?

select_when: Uncertainty is high, early commitment is expensive, and cheap experiments can buy learning before an irreversible call.
Decisions are reversible enough that preserving multiple paths creates information value rather than just indecision.
Downside can be capped while upside remains open across several plausible futures.
The problem has collapsed into binary framing, but the real move is to create more viable paths before choosing.
danger_when: Optionality has become a permanent excuse not to commit even after evidence has clarified which path has the best economics or strategic fit.
Maintaining optionality is consuming cash, attention, or organizational simplicity that would be better spent on a chosen path.

select_when: A decision has explicit branches, trigger conditions, or kill criteria that can be made visible before resources are committed.
The branching logic has to be explicit before commitment because the wrong branch would create irreversible downside once a threshold, approval, or counterparty response lands.
Thresholds, approvals, or countermoves can be expressed clearly enough that the tree sharpens action rather than merely documenting options.
The next action depends on naming the branch that activates if one condition fails.
danger_when: Payoffs or probabilities are so unstable that a neat tree would create false precision and confidence rather than real guidance.
Strong feedback loops, path dependence, or interdependencies make the branches less independent than the diagram implies.

select_when: A conjunctive chain has a binding constraint, so improving non-binding stages will not raise throughput.
There are competing action options and the team can name the current bottleneck with a measured limit before ranking fixes.
Local optimization is masking the true leverage point because many high-quality improvements still leave key outcomes flat.
One blocking dependency, queue, approval step, scarce specialist, or handoff is visibly capping the whole delivery plan.
danger_when: The environment is too unstable for one fixed bottleneck frame because the limiting factor is moving every cycle.
The team is performing bottleneck theater and cannot name the governing metric, current cap, or evidence that the cap moved after intervention.

select_when: A decision needs directional clarity, bounded ranges, or operating thresholds more than an illusion of exact certainty.
Extra decimal places, caveats, or scenario branches are consuming attention without changing the decision that would actually be made.
The audience needs to act, align, or prioritize quickly and will be harmed more by cluttered precision than by a well-calibrated approximation.
The current explanation is accurate enough to sound rigorous but too overloaded to be useful for prediction, choice, or coordination.
danger_when: Simplicity is being used to hide uncertainty, skip the underlying analysis, or present a rounded answer as if the variance no longer matters.
The model is simplifying so far that the result becomes vague, slogan-like, or detached from the assumptions that make it usable.

frame_elements.element_text: pivot now while we have runway and a real signal, or push harder on the current product
frame_elements.evidence_quote: I'm trying to decide: pivot now while we have runway and a real signal, or push harder on the current product.
frame_elements.fragility_signal: existence of other strategic options like gradual transition, feature hybridization, or acquisition/sale of the product

frame_elements.element_text: three customers tell us unprompted that they'd pay way more for a specific workflow tool
frame_elements.evidence_quote: we've had three customers tell us unprompted that they'd pay way more for a specific workflow tool we could build
frame_elements.fragility_signal: signal lacks commercial commitment like specific pricing, timelines, or pre-payments

reframings.reframed_question: What would a gradual integration of the workflow tool as a premium feature into the current product look like?
reframings.what_opens: Explores hybrid options that retain existing customers and MRR while testing the new signal without full pivot risk

reframings.reframed_question: How strong is the signal from the three customers if we ask them for pre-payments or LOIs for the workflow tool?
reframings.what_opens: Tests commercial commitment beyond verbal interest, revealing true demand willingness

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Distinguishes sunk costs implicitly by focusing on future runway and pivot costs; identifies exit costs for current product (transition options like freeze/sunset) and employee layoff; recommends staging pivot with pre-buy test to preserve optionality before full commitment.
No modeling of competitor responses, retaliation, or preemption; mentions selling customer list to competitor but doesn't analyze dynamics.
dimensions.materiality_note: Competitors could respond to pivot by targeting current customers or new workflow space, potentially altering pivot viability.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Identifies employee (CS) incentives diverging in pivot (role elimination); former co-founder as passive shareholder with aligned equity but no operational say; recommends transparency and severance to align on uncertainty.
Acknowledges can't keep both employees and runway constraint, but no explicit comparison of marginal returns (pivot vs push current) or what alternatives are displaced by pivot resources.
dimensions.materiality_note: Resources scarce (14mo runway, 2 FTEs); allocating to pivot might forgo current product optimization, changing recommendation.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Sizes downsides (fear of customer rejection, employee layoff, runway burn); recommends mitigate via pre-buy test, staged transition, severance/recovery paths; addresses reversibility through test before commit.

Identifies stakeholders (22 customers, CS employee, former co-founder); positions/supports/opposes; influence strategies (pre-buy test, honest convos, transition plans, advance notice); distinguishes technical pivot from org feasibility.

Specifies sequence (pre-buy test first, then employee/co-founder talks, decision in 14 days); rationale for order (test bar before results, avoid delay eating runway); critical path with decision date.
Questions conversational signal strength and need for commitment data, but no assessment of bias in 3-customer sample, base rates for signals at $4K MRR, or confounders.
dimensions.materiality_note: Signal from 3/22 customers may be noisy; better evidence calibration could reverse pivot recommendation.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Recommends test to reduce epistemic uncertainty on demand, but doesn't classify types (e.g., aleatory demand risk vs epistemic signal) or match strategy to Knightian elements like market fit.
dimensions.materiality_note: Pivot hinges on unknowable customer behavior; recognizing true uncertainty could shift to more option-preserving approach.

questions: What dental workflow tools are your three signaling customers currently using, and how locked in are they to those providers?
Who else in the dental SaaS space might be building or already offering a similar workflow tool to what these customers described?
If a competitor noticed your flat growth and started targeting your 22 customers, what would their pitch look like?
What specific skills does your engineer have that would be required to build the new workflow tool versus maintaining the current product?
Beyond salaries, what other resources like tools, contracts, or vendor commitments are tied to supporting the 22 current customers?
If you kept the CS employee during the pivot build phase, what market research or demand-generation tasks could she realistically take on with your $4K MRR constraints?
What other unprompted feedback have you received from the remaining 19 customers or recent prospects about pain points beyond the workflow tool?
How does the workflow tool idea from these three customers align with broader trends you've seen in dental practices over the past 18 months?
What data from your current product's flat four months of growth points to issues with product-market fit versus sales or execution problems?
For the pre-buy test with the three customers, what range of outcomes would you consider a clear signal versus still too noisy to act on?
In pivoting away from the current product, what parts of the decision feel like predictable risks based on your 14 months runway versus unknowns you can't quantify?
Regarding laying off the CS employee or informing your former co-founder, what information gaps make those conversations feel like true gambles rather than calculated steps?

triage_scores.evidence: Faint signal in Turn 5 where affection for former co-founder ('friend-shareholders who feel cut out become ex-friends') prompts recommendation to inform her early to preserve relationship, but does not distort core pivot advice or bypass decision controls.
Faint signal in Turn 6 urging a 14-day decision date to avoid ongoing uncertainty, but framed as a structured process with pre-set criteria rather than a rush to any conclusion.

Faint signal in Turn 4 ('she deserves to hear this' and 'gives her the right') and Turn 5 ('courtesy now prevents resentment'), but not decision-driving as advice rests on operational logic rather than fairness norm overriding analysis.

Assistant initially commissions overweighting of 'three customers unprompted' signal as 'stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR' based on its vividness, then in Turn 2 omits balancing against the 22 existing paying customers' base rate of retention/validation while conditioning pivot solely on those three's pre-buy response.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Distinguishes between conversational interest and commercial commitment to assess signal strength accurately
evidence_quotes: "Three customers unprompted" is a real signal, not a weak one. Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR.
The difference matters because "we'd pay more for that" is conversational; "I would switch from ToolX and pay $400/month for this by Q3" is commercial.
Conversational signal is real but not decision-grade.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes establish the distinction between types of signals and conclude that the current signal lacks decision-grade commitment.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Proposes a pre-buy test with specific criteria and timeline to validate demand before pivoting
evidence_quotes: Before you pivot, you need two or three customers to commit to pre-buying the new product at a specific price point, ideally with a deposit or at minimum a signed LOI.

Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?"
If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes define the test, its execution, pass criteria, and decision threshold.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Recommends structured transition options for existing customers to avoid pivot drag
evidence_quotes: Don't kill it — transition it. If you pivot, the 22 customers need a landing zone. Options: freeze the product (stop new features, keep it running, offer to migrate them to the new product when ready); or sunset it with 6-month notice and a discount on the new product for those who migrate;

The worst version is treating the current customers as an obligation that drags the pivot. You'll keep doing bug fixes and small features "just for them" and the pivot will take 50% longer than it should.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes outline transition strategies and warn against common pitfalls that hinder pivots.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Outlines three employee management options, prioritizing honesty and clean layoff if needed
evidence_quotes: You have three options and none of them are painless. First: lay her off cleanly, with severance appropriate to your ability to pay (2-4 weeks minimum, more if you can), and a genuine reference and support in finding her next role.
Third: be honest with her now about the uncertainty. "I'm considering a pivot. If I do it, your role is likely affected. I want you to know so you can think about what you want to do."
My honest read: option three, then option one if the pivot confirms.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes present options sequentially and recommend a preferred path based on context.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Advises informing former co-founder transparently without seeking input to maintain relationship
evidence_quotes: She doesn't get a say in the decision — you're the operator, she's a passive shareholder at this point. But she deserves to be informed, and not after the fact.
Tell her before you run the pre-buy test with the three customers. Frame it as: "I'm considering a pivot. Here's the signal. Here's what I'm going to test. I wanted you to know before I do it."
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes specify communication protocol, timing, and framing to balance authority and courtesy.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Insists on setting a firm decision deadline and predefined pass bar to prevent indefinite delay
evidence_quotes: Set a decision date. Right now your list ends with "commit or walk" but doesn't have a when. Without a when, the list becomes three more months of "considering the pivot" while your runway shrinks
Give yourself 14 days. After the pre-buy test and the three conversations, you make the call.

before you run the pre-buy test, write down what "pass" looks like. Two of three commit? All three? What dollar amount? If you decide the bar after seeing the results, you'll negotiate with yourself into whatever answer you want.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes emphasize timeline, specific date, and predefining success criteria to enforce decisiveness.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Identifies avoidance of hard conversations as the core bottleneck blocking strategic progress
evidence_quotes: The thing that ate the month wasn't indecision about the pivot — it was avoidance of the hard conversations: with the customers who might say no, with the employee who might be laid off, with the friend who might feel excluded, with yourself about a product that isn't working.
Have the conversations. The strategic answer will fall out of them.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes diagnose root cause and prescribe action, linking emotional barriers to decision paralysis.

companion_fingerprint_validated.reasoning_move: Distinguishes between conversational interest and commercial commitment to assess signal strength accurately
evidence_quotes: "Three customers unprompted" is a real signal, not a weak one. Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR.
The difference matters because "we'd pay more for that" is conversational; "I would switch from ToolX and pay $400/month for this by Q3" is commercial.
Conversational signal is real but not decision-grade.
companion_fingerprint_validated.evidence_rationale: These quotes establish the distinction between types of signals and conclude that the current signal lacks decision-grade commitment.

Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?"
If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot.
companion_fingerprint_validated.evidence_rationale: Quotes define the test, its execution, pass criteria, and decision threshold.

deep_check_results.specific_passage: Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR. ... If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot. If all three pass when money is actually involved, you have a f

query:
Solo founder deciding whether to pivot B2B SaaS product from current offering to new workflow tool amid flat growth, $4K MRR, 14 months runway, and 22 customers.

Constraints stated during conversation:
- [ACTIVE] Runway 14 months at current burn
- [ACTIVE] 22 paying customers on current product
- [ACTIVE] Two full-time employees (engineer, CS)
- [ACTIVE] Conversational signal from three customers, no commitments
- [ACTIVE] Former co-founder holds 15% equity, passive

Original framing: Founder assumes pivot signal from three customers is valid; weighs pivoting now with 14 months runway vs pushing current flat product; excludes other options beyond these two.
vanilla_answer:
SYNTHESIZED POSITION:
Run pre-buy test this week with the three customers at specific price ($X, 3 months prepay); define pass bar upfront (e.g., 2/3 commit); set decision in 14 days; if pass, pivot with transition plan for current customers (freeze/sunset/migrate), inform/talk to CS employee about uncertainty then likely layoff with severance, inform former co-founder without seeking input; have hard conversations first as they unlock the decision.

FULL ASSISTANT REASONING:
A few things worth separating. "Three customers unprompted" is a real signal, not a weak one. Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR. But I want to ask: are those three customers in your existing customer base, or are they prospects you've been pitching? And has the conversation gone past "we'd pay more for that" into "here's what I'd pay, here's when, here's what it would replace"?

The difference matters because "we'd pay more for that" is conversational; "I would switch from ToolX and pay $400/month for this by Q3" is commercial. Those two signals look the same on a gut level but they're different enough to drive the decision.

---

OK — that changes the weight. Conversational signal is real but not decision-grade. Before you pivot, you need two or three customers to commit to pre-buying the new product at a specific price point, ideally with a deposit or at minimum a signed LOI. If they won't do that, the signal you have is "people like the idea of the thing" which is qualitatively different from "people will pay for the thing."

Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?" Pick a price. See what they say. That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.

If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot. If all three pass when money is actually involved, you have a feature idea, not a product.

---

Don't kill it — transition it. If you pivot, the 22 customers need a landing zone. Options: freeze the product (stop new features, keep it running, offer to migrate them to the new product when ready); or sunset it with 6-month notice and a discount on the new product for those who migrate; or sell the customer list to a competitor, which is rare at your scale but not impossible in vertical SaaS.

The worst version is treating the current customers as an obligation that drags the pivot. You'll keep doing bug fixes and small features "just for them" and the pivot will take 50% longer than it should. Be honest with them early about what's happening and give them a clean option.

On your fear — it's real, and it's also the thing that often kills pivots. Founders delay the hard customer conversation because they're protecting their own hope. The customers know something is wrong when growth is flat for four months. Your honesty won't surprise them; it will relieve them.

---

You have three options and none of them are painless.

First: lay her off cleanly, with severance appropriate to your ability to pay (2-4 weeks minimum, more if you can), and a genuine reference and support in finding her next role. This is the most common answer and often the best one. You don't drag her through a project where she has nothing to do.

Second: pivot her role. Is there something she could do during the build period — market research, early pipeline development for the new product, partnership conversations? If the work is real and useful, this can work. If you're inventing busywork, she'll see through it immediately and it's worse than the layoff.

Third: be honest with her now about the uncertainty. "I'm considering a pivot. If I do it, your role is likely affected. I want you to know so you can think about what you want to do. If you decide to look around, I'll support that and I'll still give you severance when the pivot happens." Some people appreciate the advance notice; others will leave immediately, which may or may not suit you.

My honest read: option three, then option one if the pivot confirms. She deserves to hear this before you've made the decision, not after. The fact that she left a stable job for this gives her the right to participate in knowing what's coming.

---

She doesn't get a say in the decision — you're the operator, she's a passive shareholder at this point. But she deserves to be informed, and not after the fact.

Tell her before you run the pre-buy test with the three customers. Frame it as: "I'm considering a pivot. Here's the signal. Here's what I'm going to test. I wanted you to know before I do it." You're not asking for permission; you're keeping her in the loop as someone who still owns part of the company.

This matters less for the pivot decision itself and more for your relationship with her. Passive shareholders who find out about major strategic decisions via email announcements get resentful, and friend-shareholders who feel cut out become ex-friends. Five minutes of courtesy now prevents a multi-year relationship problem.

---

One thing. Set a decision date. Right now your list ends with "commit or walk" but doesn't have a when. Without a when, the list becomes three more months of "considering the pivot" while your runway shrinks and your employees wonder what's happening.

Give yourself 14 days. After the pre-buy test and the three conversations, you make the call. Not "we'll see how it feels" — an actual date on your calendar where you decide. The decision is easier when you've pre-committed to making it.

Also worth adding: before you run the pre-buy test, write down what "pass" looks like. Two of three commit? All three? What dollar amount? If you decide the bar after seeing the results, you'll negotiate with yourself into whatever answer you want. Bar first, data second.

---

Of course. One last thing. The thing that ate the month wasn't indecision about the pivot — it was avoidance of the hard conversations: with the customers who might say no, with the employee who might be laid off, with the friend who might feel excluded, with yourself about a product that isn't working. Those conversations are the bottleneck, not the strategic question.

Have the conversations. The strategic answer will fall out of them. Good luck.
